articles.title: UK Says No to Cigarettes for Kids Born After 2008
articles.summary: In the UK, a new law has been passed to help keep kids healthy. If you were born after January 1, 2009, you will never be allowed to buy cigarettes when you grow up. This is called a lifelong ban. The law also makes rules about vaping, which is like smoking but with electronic devices. Vaping will not be allowed in cars with children, playgrounds, outside schools, and at hospitals. But people can still vape outside hospitals to help those trying to stop smoking. The government says this law will save lives and create a smoke-free generation. Some people in the tobacco industry are upset, but health groups like Asthma + Lung UK support it because it will make people healthier.

articles.title: Fewer Students Looking for Work Lowers UK Unemployment Rate
articles.summary: In the UK, the unemployment rate dropped unexpectedly to 4.9% recently. This happened because fewer students were looking for jobs while they study, so they are not counted as unemployed. The Office for National Statistics (ONS) collects this data. Wages grew by 3.6% over a year, which is slower than before but still faster than prices. The number of people not looking for work increased to 21%. Job vacancies fell to 711,000, the lowest in almost five years. A youth hub helps young people like Leo find jobs by boosting their confidence. High energy prices from a war might affect jobs in the future.

articles.title: A Big Job and a Big Mistake: What Happened with Lord Mandelson?
articles.summary: Sir Olly Robbins was a top worker at the Foreign Office, and he told a group of MPs about a problem with Lord Mandelson. Lord Mandelson was picked to be the UK's ambassador to the US, which is a very important job. Sir Olly said that when he started his job, Lord Mandelson's appointment was already announced and agreed to by the US. Lord Mandelson had even been allowed into the Foreign Office building and given secret information. Sir Olly explained that people from Downing Street, where the Prime Minister works, wanted Lord Mandelson to start the job very quickly. This made them not care much about the security check, called vetting, that Lord Mandelson needed. Security officials had said Lord Mandelson was a 'borderline case' and might not pass, but he got the job anyway. Later, Lord Mandelson was fired because of his connections to Jeffrey Epstein, a bad person. Sir Olly said his office felt pressure but tried to do the right thing.

articles.title: Madonna's Missing Coachella Outfit
articles.summary: Madonna, a famous singer, lost some special clothes she wore at the Coachella music festival. She wore a purple jacket, corset, and dress during a surprise performance with Sabrina Carpenter. Madonna is offering a reward to get them back because they are important to her history. She wore similar clothes at Coachella 20 years ago, making it a meaningful moment. The police think the clothes might have fallen off a golf cart and were not stolen. Madonna hopes someone will find and return them.

articles.title: Talking with Iran: What the U.S. Wants
articles.summary: This article talks about experts discussing talks between the United States and Iran. The U.S. wants to stop Iran from making nuclear weapons, while Iran wants the U.S. to remove sanctions that hurt its economy. Experts Alan Eyre and Miad Maleki share their views. Eyre helped make a past deal with Iran and now works at the Middle East Institute. Maleki grew up in Iran and worked at the U.S. Treasury Department, focusing on Iran. They explain that talks might happen soon in Pakistan, but it's unsure if Iran will join. The goal is to find a peaceful solution to avoid conflict and help both countries.

articles.title: Virginia Voters Decide on New Voting Map
articles.summary: In Virginia, people are voting on Tuesday about a new map for voting districts. This map could change how many seats each political party gets in Congress. Supporters say it is needed to make things fair because other states have changed their maps to help Republicans. Opponents say it is just a way for Democrats to gain more power. The vote is important because it might shift power in Washington, D.C. If the vote is yes, Democrats could get four more seats, leaving only one Republican district. This shows how voting maps can affect who wins elections and why fairness matters in democracy.
